coredata.dc:title: Lanthanum niobate perovskite thin films for Li-ion microbatteries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2026 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Touré, Oumar
Le Granvalet, Maryline
Wernert, Romain
Bodet, Maxime
Petit, Pierre-Emmanuel
Besland, Marie-Paule
Brousse, Thierry
Barbé, Jérémy
coredata.dc:description: The development of high-performance microbatteries for the Internet of Things (IoT) and smart devices applications requires anode materials that combine a high energy density with fast charge/discharge capabilities. In this study, we report on the synthesis of La–Nb–O thin film anode for Li-ion high-power microbatteries. The thin films were obtained by reactive co-sputtering, which is compatible with miniaturization and microelectronic processes. By adjusting the power applied to La and Nb targets, we controlled the La/Nb atomic ratio and selectively promoted the formation of La1/3NbO3, a vacancy-rich perovskite phase. X-Ray Diffraction (XRD), Raman spectroscopy, and Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) observations reveal the formation of a mixed-phase composed of an orthorhombic La1/3NbO3 matrix and LaNbO4 grains for La/Nb ratios slightly above 1/3, while pure LaNbO4 are formed for higher La/Nb ratios. Electrochemical tests demonstrated that LaNbO4 is electrochemically inactive while La1/3NbO3-containing thin films exhibit high areal and gravimetric capacities (up to 73 μAh cm−2 and 203 mAh g−1, respectively) and good rate capability and reversibility (10 μAh.cm−2 in 200 s charge or discharge). A description of Li+ insertion site and occupancy is given based on theoretical and experimental considerations, which suggests that 1.44 Li + could be inserted per La1/3NbO3 unit.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2026 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Chemical and structural characterization of thin films deposited with varying Nb target powers. (a) La/Nb atomic ratio determined by EDX and XPS. (b) XRD patterns measured in grazing incidence configuration as a function of Nb target power. (c) Raman spectra measured between 50 and 1100 cm−1 with 514 nm laser as a function of power applied on the Nb target. (d) XPS analysis of La 3d and Nb 3p core levels reflecting compositional and chemical state changes as a function of Nb target power.

(a) cross-section and (b) top-view scanning electron microscopy images of La-Nb-O thin films deposited on Si/SiO2/Cr/Pt substrates as a function of Nb content. (c) Atomic force microscopy images measured on 10 × 10 μm2 area.

Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) images and elemental analysis of Samples Nb150 and Nb300. (a) and (b) High-Angle Annular Dark Field (HAADF) images and elemental mapping (green = Nb, magenta = La) measured by EDX of samples Nb150 and Nb300, respectively. (c) Atomic fraction and error values of elements (O, Nb, La) in different areas of the samples. (d) FFT and selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns for the marked regions. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) HAADF observation of Nb300 sample along its [100] zone axe. The image shows different orientations within grains of Nb300 (b) and (c) Fast Fourrier Transformed (FFT) patterns of areas 1 and 2 showing a superstructure along [001]∗ direction. In the [100] projection: (d) HAADF image of Nb300, (e) and (f) atomic chemical mapping of La and Nb, and (g) projection of the deficient La1/3NbO3 perovskite structure.

Electrochemical performances of La-Nb-O thin films as a function of Nb content. (a) Cyclic voltammetry measured at 0.5 mV/s of La-Nb-O films with power on the Nb target varying from 1500 W (Nb1500) to 300 W (Nb300). Expanded current scale for low Nb content is shown in SI figure S7, (b) Areal discharge capacity of La-Nb-O thin films as a function of Nb target power measured by CV at 0.5 mV s−1. (c) Areal discharge capacity of La-Nb-O thin films as a function of La1/3NbO3 molar percentage at 0.5 mV s−1. (d) Galvanostatic charge/discharge curves of Nb300 sample measured with constant current of 0.42 μA (6 cycles).

Rate performance of Nb300 sample. (a) Areal discharge capacity calculated by using cyclic voltammetry measurement. (b) Cyclic voltammetry curves of Nb300 sample measured from 0.1 to 10 mV s−1 and returning to 0.1 mV s−1.

Effect of film thickness. (a) Cross-sectional SEM images of La1/3NbO3 thin films (Nb300) with deposition time varying from 10 to 40 min. (b) Thickness as a function of deposition time. (c) Evolution of areal capacity as a function of film thickness. (d) Cyclic voltammetry curves measured at 0.5 mV.s-1 of Nb300 samples with thickness of 500 nm (10 min), 1500 nm (30 min) and 2000 nm (40 min). (e, f) Galvanostatic charge/discharge curves of Nb300 sample with thickness of 500 nm and 1500 nm measured with constant current of 0.42 μA and 1 μA respectively.

Structural model of pristine (left) and Li-inserted (right) La1/3NbO3 with the 3 different types of Li window sites that can be occupied. Li1 site maximum occupancy per formula unit is 1, Li2 site is 1/3 and Li3 site is 1/9. The maximum fraction of Li+ is thus equal to 1.44 Li+ per formula unit.
Experimental conditions for lanthanum niobate thin films deposition and film thickness.
Bond valence sum for the different Li insertion sites.

head.ce:title: Lanthanum niobate perovskite thin films for Li-ion microbatteries

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institut des Matériaux de Nantes Jean Rouxel

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of high-performance microbatteries for the Internet of Things (IoT) and smart devices applications requires anode materials that combine a high energy density with fast charge/discharge capabilities. In this study, we report on the synthesis of La–Nb–O thin film anode for Li-ion high-power microbatteries. The thin films were obtained by reactive co-sputtering, which is compatible with miniaturization and microelectronic processes. By adjusting the power applied to La and Nb targets, we controlled the La/Nb atomic ratio and selectively promoted the formation of La1/3NbO3, a vacancy-rich perovskite phase. X-Ray Diffraction (XRD), Raman spectroscopy, and Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) observations reveal the formation of a mixed-phase composed of an orthorhombic La1/3NbO3 matrix and LaNbO4 grains for La/Nb ratios slightly above 1/3, while pure LaNbO4 are formed for higher La/Nb ratios. Electrochemical tests demonstrated that LaNbO4 is electrochemically inactive while La1/3NbO3-containing thin films exhibit high areal and gravimetric capacities (up to 73 μAh cm−2 and 203 mAh g−1, respectively) and good rate capability and reversibility (10 μAh.cm−2 in 200 s charge or discharge). A description of Li+ insertion site and occupancy is given based on theoretical and experimental considerations, which suggests that 1.44 Li + could be inserted per La1/3NbO3 unit.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The development of high-performance microbatteries is critical for powering the rapidly growing Internet of Things (IoT) devices, which demand energy-efficient storage solutions with both high energy and power densities [1–3]. Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs), typically employing graphite as the anode material, are widely used in such applications. Graphite allows the insertion of lithium at an average potential close to that of Li+/Li (0.1 V vs. Li+/Li). However, fast charging rates can lead to the formation of metallic Li, plating as dendrites, resulting in possible short circuits and thermal runaway. Furthermore, graphite's specific capacity significantly decreases at high current densities. This is due to kinetic limitations in ion diffusion between graphene layers.

In the search for better anode materials working at high rate, spinel Li4Ti5O12 has gained attention but is constrained by a lower capacity compared to graphite, and a high working potential vs. Li+/Li, which limits its energy output at the full-cell level [4–7]. Other materials, such as shear-structured compounds like TiNb2O7 have shown promising performance, but face similar challenges related to their performance under high rates [8,9].

To overcome these drawbacks, one of the most widely adopted strategies has been the reduction of particle size to the nanoscale, which minimizes ion diffusion distances and enhances performance. However, nanocrystallization can negatively impact volumetric packing density, increase manufacturing costs, and undermine the long-term stability and sustainability of the materials.

Challenges are even higher when trying to translate the performances of bulk electrodes to micro-devices. Graphite is difficult to deposit as thin films and there have been few attempts to use TiO2 (especially TiO2(B) polymorph) or Li4Ti5O12 as thin layers negative electrodes in lithium-ion microbatteries [10–13].

La2/3-x Li3x TiO3 has been investigated as solid-state electrolyte for the lithium-ion batteries with a bulk ionic conductivity of σ ≈ 10−3 Ω−1 cm−1 at room temperature [14,15]. Based on previous works on this material we propose an approach to facilitate efficient alkali ion insertion with minimal diffusion limitations.

Promising candidates are A-site deficient perovskites, which are characterized by intrinsic vacancies, which could organize into ion transport channels. Ln1/3NbO3 perovskite-type phases (Ln = La,Nd) initially introduced by Delmas et al., in 1988, were prepared by a solid-state reaction at high temperature [16]. High intercalation rates of 0.8 Li + per La1/3NbO3 unit formula were obtained, suggesting extra insertion site for Li+, considering 0.67 vacancies per unit. In latter work by Liu et al. [17], a superstructure related to the ordering of La3+ ions and vacancies in the A site was observed. In this case, it was demonstrated that approximately 0.67 lithium could be electrochemically inserted per La1/3NbO3 formula. In other works by Nakayama et al., galvanostatic measurements showed a potential drop at composition x = 0.69 in LixLa1/3NbO3, suggesting that all the vacancies were filled with only one electrochemically inserted Li+ ions [18,19]. Following this pioneering study, there was a noticeable gap in significant follow-up research until more recent years. Xue et al. reported the synthesis of various La-Nb-O compounds through solid-state reactions. Their findings demonstrated that La1/3NbO3 could accommodate lithium ions with minimal structural strain, resulting in high capacities and excellent cycling stability [20]. These studies highlight the potential of La1/3NbO3 as an anode for Li-ion batteries, yet there is disagreement among the authors regarding the Li insertion sites within the perovskite structure and the material's theoretical maximum capacity.

For micro-batteries, it is technically challenging to deposit these powders on small areas and to optimize compactness. For this purpose, thin film deposition methods such as physical vapour deposition (PVD) can be an adequate solution. Herein, A-site deficient La1/3NbO3 perovskite thin films were deposited by reactive magnetron co-sputtering of Nb and La targets in Ar/O2 plasma. Sputtering is highly compatible with miniaturization and microelectronic processes, which makes it possible to deposit homogeneous layers on small surfaces and offers the possibility to prepare interdigitated electrodes implemented in microsupercapacitor applications [21,22]. Previously, LaNbO4 and LiNbO3 were synthesized using pulsed laser deposition (PLD) for protons conducting solid electrolytes [23,24], but sputtering has never been used for depositing these materials, and La1/3NbO3 was never obtained as a thin film.

In this work, 300 nm to 2 μm-thick La1/3NbO3 perovskite thin films were obtained by adjusting the power on La and Nb targets in magnetron co-sputtering to obtain the appropriate La/Nb atomic ratio. The deposition process was followed by high-temperature annealing in air to induce crystallization of the films. A comprehensive analysis of the structural, chemical, and electrochemical properties of the thin film electrode was then conducted for high power micro-devices. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study to demonstrate the deposition of LaNbO4 and La1/3NbO3 thin films via sputtering. The results show that samples predominantly composed of La1/3NbO3 exhibit high specific capacity and good rate performance, while the LaNbO4 phase is found to be inactive.

Thin films deposition were performed on silicon wafers with 500 nm-thermal SiO2 layer by reactive magnetron co-sputtering (Ar/O2) from pure 4 in. lanthanum (purity 99.9%) and niobium (purity 99.9%) targets. Before deposition, the pressure was kept below 10−6 mbar and the target–substrate distance was fixed at 10 cm. The depositions were performed with 80 sccm of Ar and 1.5 sccm of O2 as reactive gas, which allows remaining in the metallic regime (cf. Fig. S1) [25]. The pressure during deposition was maintained at 5.10−3 mbar. The substrate holder was rotating to obtain a homogenous composition over the substrate. For this study, the power applied on the lanthanum target was fixed at 150 W using a RF power supply, while the power applied on the niobium target was varied between 100 W and 300 W using a DC power supply, as described in Table 1 . The deposition time was fixed at 10 min, except for the study of the thickness for which the deposition time was varied between 10 and 40 min. After deposition, the as deposited thin films were ex-situ annealed at 1000°C during 2 h under air atmosphere in a muffle furnace.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) observations were conducted on a ZEISS Merlin instrument equipped with a secondary electron in-lens detector and operating at an acceleration voltage of 20 kV. Besides, Energy-Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDS-oxford instruments detector) analysis were performed for analysis of the chemical elemental composition and distribution within the sample.

The analysis of the surface chemical composition was carried out by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using a Kratos Nova photoelectron spectrometer equipped with a monochromatic Al Kα source (1486.6 eV) operating at 20 mA and 15 kV. The surface analyzed was about 700 × 300 μm2 and the instrument base pressure was 5 × 10−9 Torr. The relevant arbon (C 1s), Oxygen (O 1s), Lanthanum (La 3d) and Niobium (Nb 3p) core levels were recorded to quantify the elemental atomic contents, using sensitivity factors from database. The binding energy scale was calibrated with the C 1s peak. Peak contributions were extracted by fitting peaks shapes with a combination of Lorentzian and Gaussian functions. The background was subtracted using a Shirley function. Casa XPS software was used to fit the high-resolution core-level spectra (Copyright © 2022 Casa Software Ltd, version 2.3.24). The two core levels La 3d and Nb 3p are not the most suitable to estimate accurately the La/Nb ratio. Indeed, the difference in binding energy is quite high (more than 500 eV) and the atomic content of the La 3d can be overestimated as compared to the Nb content. Another possibility would have been to choice two different core levels, i.e. La 4p and Nb 3d, with closer binding energy values ranging in a smaller range of values (210 and 180 eV). Nevertheless, in that case, an overlap occurs between the two doublets, leading to more uncertainty in the atomic content calculation. La 3d and Nb 3p appear thus as the best compromise in this study. We should remind that without reference LaNbO4 and La1/3NbO3 materials, the calculated atomic contents should only be considered as relative values.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analyses were performed using a Malvern Panalytical Empyrean diffractometer operating with Cu Kα1,2 radiation, operating at 45 kV and 40 mA. Since thin films were deposited on silicon wafers, the substrate contribution often dominated over that of the thin film. To minimize the substrate contribution in the X-ray patterns, measurements were performed using a grazing incidence X-ray diffraction (GIXRD) configuration with a fixed incidence angle of 2° and a parallel beam, using a parabolic mutilayered mirror at the exit of the tube and long equatorial Soller slits between the sample and the detector (used in 0-D mode). Rietveld refinements were performed with Topas v7 software. The reported structural models from orthorhombic La1/3NbO3, monoclinic LaNbO4 and tetragonal LaNbO4 were used to refine the weight ratio of the different phases. A spherical harmonic correction of order 4 was applied when needed to account for preferred orientation.

Raman spectroscopy analyses were performed using a Jobin-Yvon Horiba T64000 spectrometer equipped with an Ar+ laser (λ = 514 nm radiation). Measurements were carried out using an 1800 gr/mm grating, a 100 μm entrance slit, a 100 μm confocal hole, and a laser power of 75 mW at the sample. Spectra were recorded using a ×100 objective, with an acquisition time of 2 × 10 s for each area.

Cross sections of thin films were prepared by using a Focused Ion Beam coupled with Scanning Electron Microscopy (FIB-SEM ZEISS crossbeam 550L). A conductive layer of platinum (Pt) was first deposited on top of the samples before FIB milling to prevent charging effects. Lamella extraction was then carried out using a lift-out procedure with Ga+ ions accelerated at 30 kV, using beam currents of 7–1.5 nA, followed by thinning at 30 kV with 300–50 pA. Samples were analyzed by Transmission Electron Microscopy (S/TEM Themis, Thermo Fisher Scientific, 300 kV) using High-Angle Annular Dark Field STEM (HAADF) images and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) for atomic- and nanometer-scale elemental mapping.

By knowing the thickness and surface area of thin films deposited through a 3 × 3 cm2 mask during deposition, the density of the deposited materials was calculated using a microbalance with a precision of 0.01 mg.

The electrochemical measurements were performed on the sputtered thin films using a flat cell configuration. A chromium layer (≈60 nm) used as adhesion layer and platinum layer (≈80 nm) used as current collector were first sputtered on Si/SiO2 substrate. Then, lanthanum niobate thin films were deposited onto it. The final structure of the working electrode is then: Si/SiO2/Cr/Pt/La-Nb-O. Glass fiber filters (Whatman GF/D) were used as the separator. The electrolyte was 1 M LiPF6, which was dissolved in dimethyl carbonate (DMC) and ethylene carbonate (EC) of equal volumes (1:1). To complete the cell a metal lithium was used as counter and reference electrode. The complete stack and SEM cross-section image are shown in Fig. S2. Cyclic voltammetry, chronoamperometry, and galvanostatic charge/discharge measurements were performed using a VMP3 potentiostat (BioLogic), operated under EC-Lab software (version V11.50), in an air-conditioned room maintained at 22°C.

The general strategy used in this study to obtain the vacancy-rich La1/3NbO3 phase with minimal impurity content involves adjusting the power applied to the Nb target while maintaining a constant power on the La target, in order to reach the desired La/Nb atomic ratio of 1/3. To evaluate the La and Nb content in the as-deposited films, energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) measurements were performed, and results are shown in Fig. 1 (a). At a Nb target power of 100 W, the La/Nb ratio is approximately 1.1. This ratio gradually decreases with increasing Nb power, reaching a value of 0.38 at 300 W, which is close to the targeted ratio of 0.33. It should be noted that EDX analyses were conducted over a relatively large area (10 × 10 μm2), providing an average composition over multiple grains; however, local variations in composition between individual grains may be present, as we will see below.

The surface composition of the films was further investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), revealing a similar trend to that observed by EDX. However, at a Nb target power of 100 W, the La/Nb atomic ratio measured by XPS is approximately 2.1, nearly twice the value obtained by EDX. As already said in the experimental section, it is worth noting that La 3d and Nb 3p regions are not the most suitable to estimate accurately the La/Nb ratio. That can be also the origin of the higher value obtained for La-rich films. At higher powers (250–300 W), the XPS-derived ratios remain slightly above those measured by EDX. Since XPS analysis probes only the very surface of the film (typically ∼10 nm), these differences likely reflect variations in elemental composition between the surface and the bulk, which could be due to phase segregation across the film. It is worth noting that the La content calculation with the La 3d peaks may overestimate it.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the annealed films (1000°C in air for 2 h) with varying Nb/La ratios are presented in Fig. 1(b). At a Nb target power of 100 W, a single-phase scheelite-type LaNbO4 with tetragonal symmetry (PDF 50-0919, space group I41/a) is formed. Increasing the Nb power to 150 W induces a phase transition to monoclinic LaNbO4 (PDF 22-1125, space group I2/b). In both LaNbO4 polymorphs, La atoms are eightfold coordinated by oxygen, while Nb atoms are in tetrahedral coordination, that should leave no space for Li+ ions intercalation [20]. At higher Nb powers (175–300 W), the emergence of the orthorhombic La1/3NbO3 perovskite phase (PDF 72-2080, space group Cmmm) is observed. Within this range, the XRD patterns indicate the presence of a mixture of La1/3NbO3 and tetragonal/monoclinic LaNbO4 phases, with the relative intensity of the La1/3NbO3 peaks increasing progressively with Nb target power. To obtain more information on the ratios of the different phases as a function of power, the Rietveld refinements of GIXRD patterns of Nb100 to Nb300 samples were performed and the results are plotted in Fig. S3. The peaks were indexed from the orthorhombic La1/3NbO3, monoclinic LaNbO4 and tetragonal LaNbO4 phases. The weight fractions of the different phases were evaluated from this analysis and plotted in Fig. S4. The results indicate that the fraction of the perovskite phase is null for Nb100 and Nb150, and then steadily increases to reach 70 wt% of La1/3NbO3 for Nb300. The formation of the La1/3NbO3 perovskite phase is thus clearly confirmed, although a significant fraction of LaNbO4 persists at a power of 300 W. The power applied on the Nb target could not be increased further 300 W and for this study it was decided to keep the La target power constant. Hence the maximum weight fraction obtained here is 70 wt% of La1/3NbO3 (o) and 30 wt% of LaNbO4 (t + m). This corresponds to a molar fraction of 80 mol.% and 20 mol.%, considering molar mass of 186 g mol−1 and 296 g mol−1 for La1/3NbO3 and LaNbO4, respectively.

Due to its high sensitivity to local atomic environments, Raman spectroscopy analysis allow to evidence variations of vibration peaks intensity associated to the different phases. Fig. 1(c) shows the Raman spectra of thin films deposited at different powers applied on the Nb target, measured after annealing. The Raman bands can be linked to the presence of two distinct niobium oxide environments. The peaks observed at 320 cm−1 and 810 cm−1 can be attributed to the symmetric Nb–O stretching modes of the NbO4 tetrahedra, which exists in the LaNbO4 phases, as described by K. Chen et al. [20]. The intensity of these peaks decreases with increasing applied power on the niobium target, suggesting a reduced amount of LaNbO4. On the other hand, the La1/3NbO3 compound include the NbO6 octahedral environments with different degree of distortions. Previous studies on niobates have shown that the NbO6 octahedral stretching modes produce strong Raman bands around 500-700 cm−1 [26,27]. It is established that structural vacancies induce significant local disorder, disturbing the long-range periodicity required for sharp Raman absorption peaks. Consequently, in this spectral range, the Nb-O stretching modes of the niobium-oxygen bond in the NbO6 octahedron, which corresponds to the La1/3NbO3 perovskite phase, is broadened across the range 500-700 cm−1. Fig. 1(c) shows an increase in the intensity of this band, suggesting the formation of vacancy-rich perovskite phase between 200 W and 300 W, in agreement with XRD results.

The XPS La 3d and Nb 3p core levels of thin films with varying Nb contents measured after annealing are shown in Fig. 1(d). It is observed that the La and Nb cations remain in La3+ and Nb5+ oxidation states for all samples. This is coherent with the presence of LaNbO4 or La1/3NbO3, which have both La3+ and Nb5+ oxidation states in pristine state. The fact that Nb can be tetrahedrally or octahedrally coordinated to oxygen in the two different phases seems not to impact the Nb binding energy.

Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) was used to investigate the microstructure and surface morphology of the annealed thin films on Si/SiO2/Cr/Pt, as shown in Fig. 2 (a) and (b). In cross-sectional views, it is observed that large grains are predominantly located in the upper region of the film, while the lower region seems composed of smaller grains. Films appear rather porous with voids visible between grains. The thickness of the films was determined from cross-section images. An almost linear increase from 275 nm to 553 nm is measured when the Nb target power increases from 100 W to 300 W, as shown in SI figure S5. Indeed, as the power on the Nb target increases, the Nb sputtering rate increases, which directly impacts the deposition rate. The bright layer is the 80 nm-thick platinum layer used as current collector for electrochemical characterisations, as described below.

From top view images, the films exhibit a surface composed of large, well-defined grains at low and medium Nb target powers (150–200 W). As the power increases to 225 W, it seems that the morphology evolves into a mixed structure: two groups of bright and dark grains could suggest the presence of two distinct phases. From 275 W, the dark grains become increasingly dominant, and at 300 W the film appears uniformly dark, with no visible bright grains and less distinguishable grain boundaries. This can be correlated with the XRD and Raman results described in Fig. 1. As the Nb content increases, the La1/3NbO3 and LaNbO4 phases can co-exist in the film (Nb175 to Nb250), until the La1/3NbO3 phase becomes predominant in the film at the expense of LaNbO4 phase (Nb275 and Nb300). Although secondary electrons imaging is not sensitive to atomic number and phase, the La1/3NbO3 and LaNbO4 phases may have different charging properties and grain morphology, which could explain the contrast in the image. It cannot be evidenced by EDX since the spatial resolution is larger than the grain size in SEM.

As a complementary characterization technique, atomic force microscopy (AFM) images of La-Nb-O thin films, acquired over 10 × 10 μm2 areas, are presented in Fig. 2(c). The AFM results are in excellent agreement with the top-view scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images. In the range of 150 to 250 W, the thin films are well crystallized, with large, distinct grains. Sample Nb250 has the largest roughness (Rq = 45 nm), which may indicate a mixed phase material as evidenced by the presence of both LaNbO4 and La1/3NbO3 in XRD patterns. As the Nb content is increased (from 275 W), the crystal grains become less distinguishable, and the films adopt a more continuous morphology; however, some pinholes remain visible.

Fig. 3 presents transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis of samples Nb150 and Nb300, for which very distinct structural characteristics were obtained. In Fig. 3(a), the HAADF (high-angle annular dark field) image of sample Nb150 shows a uniform thin film with a few pinholes and no clearly visible grain boundaries. X-ray energy-dispersive (EDX) analysis reveals that Nb and La are uniformly distributed throughout the film, with the atomic fraction measured of La and Nb measured in Area 1 corresponding to LaNbO4 (Fig. 3(c)). Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) diagrams of the same zone 1, shown in Fig. 3(d), indicate that the film is well crystallized, with spots evidencing a spatial periodicity of 6.16 nm−1 corresponding to the (
1 ‾
21) crystallographic plane of monoclinic LaNbO4, with an interplanar distance of 0.325 nm.

In Fig. 3(b), the HAADF image of sample Nb300 reveals a biphasic material, with bright grains embedded in a dark grey matrix. EDX analysis in areas 2 and 3 indicate that the bright grains exhibit a La/Nb ratio of 1, which correspond to LaNbO4, while the grey matrix, with a La/Nb ratio of 0.33, corresponds perfectly to the La1/3NbO3 phase. The La1/3NbO3 appears darker in HAADF due to the lower amount of La, which has a high atomic number. Fig. 3(d) shows FFT patterns of areas 2 and 3. A distance of 6.22 nm−1 is measured in area 2, which would correspond to the (
1 ‾
21) crystallographic plane of monoclinic LaNbO4 phase (as observed in Nb150), while area 3 has a spatial periodicity of 2.52 nm−1, which corresponds to the (002) crystallographic plane of orthorhombic La1/3NbO3 with an interplanar distance of 0.396 nm.

These findings align very well with our previous observations, where a pure monoclinic LaNbO4 phase forms at 150 W power on the Nb target, while a biphasic film containing La1/3NbO3 and LaNbO4 is obtained at 300 W. For this latter, the La1/3NbO3 phase forms a continuous matrix without clear grain boundaries (70 wt% from Rietveld analysis), in which are embedded a smaller amount of LaNbO4 grains (30 wt%).

Fig. 4 focuses on the La1/3NbO3 orthorhombic perovskite structure obtained with sample Nb300. The Z-contrast HAADF image (Figure4(a)) was taken along the [100] zone axis of the structure. The atomic columns containing heavier atoms display bright spots, while oxygen and vacancies produce negligible scattering and are not visible. The atomic arrangement of the heavy elements is highly ordered, and the image clearly reveals the presence of domains with distinct orientations of the La - vacancies ordering within a given crystallite. Besides, the Fast Fourrier Transform (FFT) of area 1 and area 2 (Figure4(b)) and 4(c)) indicate a superstructure along the [001]∗ direction in both case.

This superstructure has been analyzed in more details by HAADF-STEM in area 1. The image in Fig. 4(d) reveals the position of heavy atoms, which correlates perfectly with the structure of vacancy-rich La1/3NbO3 perovskite, where La vacancies are organised in a superstructure as shown in Fig. 4(g): one La plane is fully vacant and appears black on the HAADF-STEM image (blue arrow), while the other La plane with 1/3 vacancies appears bright (red arrow) because of the absence of long range order in the distribution of vacancies within the (a,b) plane of the crystallite, which are effectively masked by La atoms.

The STEM-EDX mappings of La and Nb atoms displayed in Fig. 4(e) and (f) confirm the distribution of La and Nb elements in deficient La1/3NbO3, with a superstructure due to the alternance of vacancy-poor layers (rich in La) and fully vacant layers (empty of La) parallel to the (001)p plane (p refers to the primitive perovskite structure). These results agree well with other study by Gao et al. on LixLa(1−x)/3NbO3 with x = 0 or x = 0.04, who observed a complex crystal structure with two types of A-cation ordering, namely a long-range layer ordering in alternate (001)p planes and a short-range (intra-domain) columnar ordering within La-rich (001)p layers [28]. If the long-range ordering is confirmed here, the short-range ordering is not observed in the intensity-scan profile shown in Fig. S6, taken from the La-rich plane in Fig. 4(d).

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) within a potential window of 1 to 3 V vs Li+/Li was chosen based on the electrochemical stability window of typical Nb-based oxide materials reported in literature [29–31]. These compounds exhibit relatively high operating voltages (above 1 V vs Li+/Li) compared to graphite or metallic lithium anodes, which can limit or even suppress the formation of solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) layers and inhibit lithium dendrite growth, thereby enhancing battery safety. Fig. 5 (a) presents the cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves of La-Nb-O thin films with varying Nb content measured at 0.5 mV s−1. We note that the thickness of the films vary from 275 nm to 520 nm with the Nb target power varying from 100 W to 300 W, as shown in SI (Fig. S5). For films deposited under low Nb target powers (100–150 W), corresponding to pure LaNbO4 phases (tetragonal or monoclinic), no notable electrochemical activity is observed. Chen et al. also found that LaNbO4 powder based electrodes displayed the smallest capacity (60 mAh.g−1) and bad rate performance, as compared to other niobium oxide compounds [20]. However, in our work the intensity of the current is very low for this sample upon cyclic voltammetry tests, suggesting that LaNbO4 is very poorly active as an anode in a Li-ions cell.

The onset of an electrochemical response is observed in samples deposited with Nb target powers ranging from 200 W to 300 W, attributed to the electrochemical activity of the La1/3NbO3 perovskite phase, which is increasingly formed with increasing power as shown by XRD analysis and related Rietveld refinements. For each cycling rate, six consecutive CV cycles were recorded. All the discharge–charge profiles are nearly superimposed on each other, demonstrating the excellent electrochemical reversibility of the thin film. for the 300 W deposited sample, the high current and strong intensity of the redox peaks is likely due to the high content of La1/3NbO3 as compared to samples prepared with lower power on Nb target. Three reversible pairs of redox peaks are evidenced, the two first in reduction and oxidation are broad while the last one at the lower potential is narrow. This can indicate a mixed electrochemical behaviour with well-defined redox reactions (redox peaks) and others spread through a wide potential window (box-type shape envelope, as observed for pseudocapacitive materials). All these processes are related to the surface and bulk reduction of Nb5+ to Nb4+ and finally a Nb4+ to Nb3+ reduction. Based on previous studies [9,32], the redox peaks centered at 1.80 and 1.75 V are attributed to the Nb5+/Nb4+ redox process, while the peaks at 1.27 and 1.15 V could be attributed to the reduction of Nb4+ to Nb3+. These findings demonstrate that La1/3NbO3 thin films exhibit electrochemical activity driven by a reversible redox process at niobium sites. The cyclic voltammograms measured at 0.1 mV s−1, 0.2 mV s−1, 0.5 mV s−1 and 1 mV s−1 are shown in SI (Figs. S7–S10), and show similar trends with increased Nb contents.

Fig. 5(b) exhibits the areal capacity of sputtered thin films as a function of Nb target power for different scan rates. The capacity increases with the deposition power, i.e. with the increased presence of La1/3NbO3, until reaching a value of 16.8 μA h cm−2 for sample Nb300 at 1 mV s−1 (and 20 μA h cm−2 at 0.1 mV s−1). This areal capacitance was also plotted in Fig. 5(c) as a function of La1/3NbO3 molar fraction, which was determined by Rietveld refinement. We note that the capacity is rapidly increasing from about 70 mol.% of La1/3NbO3, whereas at 50 mol.% of La1/3NbO3 (Nb200), the capacity is almost null. This may indicate that LaNbO4 impedes charge storage in the active perovskite phase by restricting Li-ion diffusion within the biphasic material. Galvanostatic charge/discharge profiles (GCD) of the Nb300 sample for a low constant current of 0.42 μA is presented in Fig. 5(c). The obtained discharge curve is very similar to previous reports showing a steady decrease from 2.2 V to 1.2 V and then a small plateau at 1.2 V corresponding to intercalation of Li+ ions in the vacancy-rich plane [19]. The areal capacity is about 15 μA h cm−2 after 6 cycles, which is close to the areal capacity measured by CV. The discharge capacity is slightly decreasing between the 1st and 6th cycle probably due to the formation of a solid electrolyte interphase SEI.

The rate capability of Nb300 sample was tested from 0.1 to 100 mV s−1 as shown in Fig. 6 (a). A high capacity >15 μA h cm−2 remains at 2 mV s−1, which corresponds to a charge/discharge in about 15 min. Importantly, the thin film's electrochemical stability is evidenced by the recovery of the initial capacity upon returning to a scan rate of 0.1 mV s−1 after cycling up to 100 mV s−1.

The cyclic voltammetry curves of Nb300 sample measured from 0.1 to 100 mV s−1 and returning to 0.1 mV s−1 are shown in Fig. 6(b). With increasing scan rate, the anodic and cathodic peaks move to higher and lower potentials, respectively, due to the increasing polarization. Similar CV shapes are observed untill 2 mV s−1. This electrochemical signature looks like an intercalation pseudocapacitive contribution during lithiation and delithiation processes, as proposed by B. Dunn et al. [33]. However, the cycling rate is much too low to asses a non-diffusion limited intercalation process. Indeed, from 5 mV s−1, the peaks tend to widen and the CV shape broaden over a large potential window which is indicative of Li+ diffusion limited process.

To meet the energy demands of miniaturized IoT devices, it is essential to optimize the surface capacity of the electrode. A simple method to increase the areal capacity is to increase the thickness of the La1/3NbO3 thin film, which can be done by increasing the deposition time. Fig. 7 (a) display the cross-section SEM images of Nb300 samples with deposition time varying from 10 to 40 min. We observe that the microstructure remains similar (granular and porous) but the thickness linearly increases with deposition time, ranging from 520 nm to 2 μm (Fig. 7(b)). As shown in Fig. 7(c), the surface capacity increases proportionally with film thickness reaching 45 μA h/cm2 and 73 μA h/cm2 at 0.5 mV s−1 for 1.5 and 2 μm thick films, respectively. However, for the 2 μm-thick film, the CV curve shown in Fig. 7(d) becomes asymmetric and displays a distorded CV shape, similar to the CV curves observed at high cycling rates (Fig. 6(b) and 5 mV s−1) for thinner films. This behavior can probably be explained by enhanced polarization and resistive effects when the thickness exceeds 1.5 μm.

In Fig. 7(e and f), the galvanostatic charge–discharge curves recorded at low current densities (0.42 μA and 1 μA for electrode thicknesses of 500 nm and 1.5 μm, respectively) within the potential window of 3.0–1.0 V vs. Li+/Li are presented. The continuously sloping potential profiles, without well-defined plateaus, suggest lithium insertion governed by a solid solution insertion reaction. Unlike CV that were performed at 0.5 mV.s-1 (reduction in 1.1 h), the GCD plots do not show diffusion limitation for Li+ cations, thanks to slow cycling rate (C/20). The difference between the charge and discharge capacities reflects irreversible capacity loss, which is commonly attributed to the formation of a solid electrolyte interphase (SEI), or irreversibly trapped lithium ions. Both samples exhibit similar potential profiles; however, the thicker electrode delivers a higher capacity.

These results align well with recent work on niobium oxide thin film electrodes: C. Lethien et al. have obtained an areal capacity of 60 μA h/cm2 at a cycling rate of 1 mV s−1 for a 0.94 μm-thick sputtered T-Nb2O5 thin film [34]. Both compounds T-Nb2O5 and La1/3NbO3 exhibit low electronic conductivity in their pristine state due to the absence of 4d electrons [35]. However, it can be hypothesized that, compared to T-Nb2O5, La1/3NbO3 provides improved ionic diffusion pathways and enhanced structural stability. The perovskite network might offer more open ion transport channels due to the absence of 7-coordinate Nb that hinders diffusion along the c crystallographic direction.

To compare our results with literature data of powder La1/3NbO3, the gravimetric discharge capacity was derived from the areal capacity. The mass of the thin film was determined by weighing the substrate before and after deposition, and the volume of the film was determined by depositing the film through a 3 × 3 cm2 mask and measuring the thickness by cross-section SEM. Based on these values, the density of the film was determined to be 2.03 g cm−3. The density was then used to convert the surface capacity to gravimetric capacity as shown in Fig. S12. A capacity of 120 mA h. g−1 is measured at low scan rate (0.1 mV s−1) for sample Nb300 after 30 min deposition.

However, this calculation includes the mass of LaNbO4, which is not electroactive as seen earlier. To obtain the real capacity of La1/3NbO3, it is necessary to take into account the weight fraction of La1/3NbO3, which was determined to be 59 wt% for this sample from Rietveld refinements (Fig. S13). In this case, a gravimetric capacity of 203 mA h g−1 is obtained for the La1/3NbO3 phase, which is in good agreement with literature values. As a comparison, X. Xiong et al. [36] have measured a capacity of 186 mA h g−1 at 0.5C cycling rate for a vacancy-rich Li0.1La1/3NbO3 perovskite powder prepared by solid-state reaction.

The Li-ion insertion processes in La1/3NbO3 can be expressed by equation (1):
(Equation 1)
L a
1 / 3
N b O
3
+ x
L i
+
+ x
e −
→
L i
x
L a
1 / 3
N b O
3

The high capacity obtained corresponds to the insertion of 1.42 Li+ per La1/3NbO3 unit, which can appear as surprising at first sight, since one could expect the maximum insertion rate to be 0.67 if Li+ was inserted in the vacant A-sites. However, Li is far too small to occupy this 12-coordinate site and would prefer to go into the 4-coordinate square planar windows as previously suggested [16,18]. Under this hypothesis, 4 window sites can be occupied in each perovskite cage of the vacant plane (Li1 in Fig. 8 ). Besides, 2 more windows in between vacant and non-vacant planes will be occupied if there are La vacancies in the non-vacant planes (Li2), and 1 more window in the non-vacant plane if two neighbouring La atoms are missing (Li3). Combining these considerations with site multiplicities and occupancies yields a maximum fraction of Li+ equal to 1.44 Li+ (1 Li1, 1/3 Li2 and 1/9 Li3) per formula unit, thus explaining the observed behaviour. Considering incertitudes linked to weight fraction determination ( ± 5%) and thin film weighing ( ± 0.74%). This is in rather good agreement with our results and supports our assumption that 1.44 Li+ ion per Nb can be incorporated in the structure as shown in Fig. 8.

A more in-depth study based on the Bond Valence Sum (BVS, see SI for more details) analysis of the stability of the different lithium intercalation sites was carried out to confirm the Li1, Li2 and Li3 sites as preferential insertion sites as compared to the central cuboctahedral LiO12 (Li4) site in the Cmmm structure of La1/3NbO3. Table 2 shows that the BVS values of square window sites fall close to the expected value of 1 while the BVS of the Li4 cuboctahedral site clearly shows that Li is strongly under bonded and thus the occupation of this site will be disfavoured. This is due to the Li―O bond lengths averaging to values in the 1.9 to 2 Å range for Li1, Li2 and Li3 sites while the average bond length of the Li4 site is 2.73 Å.

Finally, the long-range layer ordering results in a large anisotropic diffusion network for Li+ ions, with easy diffusion along a and b but partially hindered diffusion along c that certainly impact the rate of charge/discharge process and is generally considered a likely impediment to high Li+ ions conductivity. This ordering could restrict ion mobility due to increased trapping in local potential wells [28]. These results suggest that finding ways to add disorder to the La distribution or to minimize its amount by partially substituting M6+ cation (Mo6+, W6+) for Nb5+ could enhance the intercalation kinetics of this family of compounds.

This study demonstrates the successful synthesis of La–Nb–O thin films by reactive magnetron co-sputtering, leading to the controlled formation of La1/3NbO3 perovskite and LaNbO4 phases through precise tuning of the La/Nb atomic ratio. This is the first time, to the best of our knowledge, that the A-site deficient perovskite is obtained via magnetron sputtering. The study of the deposited thin films, using structural and electrochemical characterizations reveal that LaNbO4 is electrochemically inactive, while La1/3NbO3 exhibits intercalation lithium storage behaviour. The capacity reaches 73 μA h/cm2 for 2 μm-thick film at 0.5 mV s−1 (67 min discharging time), along with good rate performance and reversibility. These results are in line with previous works on La1/3NbO3 prepared by solid-state routes, and confirm that the electrochemical response can be preserved in thin film form using sputtering, a method compatible with microelectronic integration. The gravimetric capacity based on thin film weight was estimated to 203 mAh.g−1, which corresponds to 1.42 Li + per La1/3NbO3 unit. We propose a description of Li + insertion sites on 4-coordinate square planar windows of A-sites with different occupancy rates, giving a theoretical 1.44 Li+ per unit, in very good agreement with our results. The observed long-range ordering of vacancies could restrict ion mobility due to increased trapping in local potential wells. Operando characterizations are needed to elucidate the detailed redox mechanisms of lithium insertion and limitations at high cycling rates. This work paves the way for a wide range of thin-film materials that could be produced through reactive co-sputtering, including La-Ti-O and Ti-Nb-O compounds, which are currently under investigation.

Oumar Touré: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Maryline Le Granvalet: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Romain Wernert: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Maxime Bodet: Investigation, Data curation. Pierre-Emmanuel Petit: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Data curation. Marie-Paule Besland: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Methodology. Thierry Brousse: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization. Jérémy Barbé: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank Jean-Yves Mevellec, Jonathan Hamon, Eric Gautron, Nicolas Gautier and Célia Ragone for the measurements performed using the IMN's characterisation platform PLASSMAT, Nantes, France.
